mappings.rationaleAI: Lakes are subject to lacustrine processes including erosion, deposition, and sedimentation (A.8), and the lacustrine environment covers lake basins, hydraulics, and glacial lakes (C.2). Tectonic, volcanic, and glacial lake origins relate to specific formation environments.

Deserts are shaped by eolian processes including wind erosion, sand dune dynamics, and transportation of rock debris (A.5), and the desert environment covers geomorphic processes, surficial features, and desert boundary expansion (C.5).

Rivers are shaped by fluvial processes including entrainment, transport, erosion, and deposition (A.4), and the fluvial environment covers drainage patterns, streamflow, flooding, floodplains, terraces, and deltas (C.1).

Erosion is the central geomorphic process, operating through weathering (A.1), fluvial entrainment and transport (A.4), eolian wind transport (A.5), marine wave and current action (A.6), and glacial processes (A.7). Human activities accelerate erosion rates up to 100-fold.

Soil formation results from weathering, biological activity, and climate influence (A.2), covering processes and factors in soil formation (A.2.a), classification and distribution (A.2.b), soil crusts (A.2.c), and soil erosion and deterioration (A.2.d).

Wind drives eolian processes (A.5) including transportation of rock debris (A.5.a), effects of wind transport including erosion (A.5.b), and deposition forming dunes with the role of vegetation (A.5.c).

Snowpack accumulation compacting into glacial ice connects to glacial processes of erosion, transport, and deposition (A.7), and the glacial environment (C.6) including periglacial processes and permafrost.

Floods represent peak discharge events within the fluvial environment, creating river floodplains and terraces (C.1.d), driven by riverbank overflow, and intensified by deforestation and urbanization.
The coldest continent with ice sheet dynamics directly relevant to the glacial environment (C.6) and the polar environment (C.7), including glacial erosion, deposition, and periglacial landforms.
The world's largest tropical moist broadleaf forest, directly relevant to the tropical environment of jungles and rain forests (C.8), with role in precipitation cycles and carbon storage.

Earth's largest hot desert, exemplifying the desert environment (C.5) with geomorphic features including mountains, plateaus, sand dunes, and 20,000-year savanna-desert cycles relevant to desert boundary expansion and contraction (C.5.c).
Volcanic eruptions producing lava flows, tephra, and aerosols represent volcanic-tectonic processes acting on Earth's surface features (B.3).
Seismic events cause landslides, soil liquefaction, and surface rupture, representing volcanic-tectonic processes affecting the Earth's surface (B.3).
Five interconnected freshwater lakes of glacial geological origin, relevant to lacustrine processes (A.8) and the lacustrine environment (C.2), specifically as glacial lakes (C.2.d).
Grass-dominated ecosystems covering 31-69% of Earth's land area, directly relevant to the temperate environment of woodlands and grasslands (C.9).
Tree-dominated ecosystems spanning tropical moist and dry forests (C.8) and temperate woodlands (C.9), with vegetation type and density affecting sediment yield and geomorphic processes (B.1.b).

The ocean drives marine processes including erosion and deposition of coastal materials by waves and currents (A.6), and shapes the marine environment with coastal features and submerged continental features (C.4).
The Ganges forms one of the world's largest delta systems, exemplifying river delta formation within the fluvial environment (C.1.d), with periodic flooding and floodplain dynamics.
China's longest river with periodic flooding and delta formation, relevant to streamflow and fluvial landforms (C.1.d) including floodplains and the economically productive Yangtze Delta.
Rain is the dominant freshwater delivery mechanism sustaining river systems and groundwater recharge, driving fluvial processes of entrainment, transport, erosion, and deposition (A.4).
Human settlements represent the effects of humankind on Earth's surface features through land cultivation, construction, and infrastructure development (B.1.c).
Artificial waterways constructed for drainage, irrigation, or transport, exemplifying human construction of artificial channels and dams affecting Earth's surface (B.1.c).

The Andes span three climatic divisions including dry conditions relevant to desert environments (C.5) and high-altitude glacial environments (C.6), with volcanism and erosional features shaping surface morphology.
The Himalayas contain extensive glaciers and are shaped by glacial processes (C.6), including glaciated valleys, cirques, and moraines, with hydrology feeding major river systems.
The Arctic Ocean with its sea ice dynamics is relevant to the polar environment (C.7) and glacial processes including periglacial phenomena (A.7).
A nearly landlocked sea with marine processes including coastal erosion and deposition (A.6) and marine environmental features (C.4).

Islands formed through volcanism, coral reef accretion, and sediment deposition relate to coral reefs (C.4.a.iv) and coastal features dependent on bedrock and topography including peninsulas and islands (C.4.c).
Storms cause storm surge, flooding, and wind-driven erosion and deposition, relevant to marine processes and coastal material modification (A.6).
